interactions.summary: Jay asks Jiang to assess his prediction accuracy; Jiang answers that he is good at broad geopolitical contours and weak on specifics and timing, citing Nikki Haley/J.D. Vance and the Iran-war timeline as misses.
Jay presses Jiang on why he uses secret societies, Freemasons, numerology, and Illuminati language when ordinary psychology, greed, or geopolitics may explain events without those frames.

Jiang answers that his early work did not use secret-society explanations, but standard game theory left unanswered why patterns persist and who implements them; internet research then led him into Freemasonry texts, Albert Pike, and Jacob Frank as possible schemas behind modern politics.

speaker_notes.note: SPEAKER_01 is Jay; SPEAKER_00 is Jiang. Jay's long setup spans several segments before Jiang's answer begins at seg-0030.

claims.claim: Jiang assesses himself as accurate on broad geopolitical direction but poor on timeline and specifics.
Jiang says his early strategy and civilization lectures did not discuss secret societies, but later doubts about who implements persistent patterns pushed him toward that research.
claims.temporal_scope: Chronology of Jiang's method shift, stated 2026-04-01.

claims.claim: Jiang treats texts like Albert Pike's Morals and Dogma and Jacob Frank's sayings as possible interpretive keys for modern political systems, while admitting his route into them was internet research rather than academic scholarship.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang narrows his own authority: he claims pattern sight, not clockwork prophecy.
signature_moments.source_phrase: broad contours of general geopolitical movements
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the cleanest self-limitation against the Nostradamus frame.

signature_moments.moment: Secret societies enter not as ornament but as Jiang's attempted answer to the implementation question: why do patterns persist and who keeps enacting them?
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It explains the pressure point where standard geopolitics stops satisfying him.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's phrase for the general geopolitical direction he thinks he can read better than specific timelines or personnel choices.
The problem of who actually carries out persistent geopolitical patterns and why they keep recurring.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang says his secret-society emphasis emerged after earlier lectures on strategy/civilization and within the last couple of years before this 2026 interview.

uncertainty_notes.note: Jiang's claim that Pike or Frank texts map onto twentieth- and twenty-first-century politics is his interpretive reading, not established evidence in this packet.
